ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 11713004 refers to water as a substance; used as closest parent as no specific mineral water product code exists in SNOMED CT.

main_ingredients: dissolved minerals (calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, trace elements)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml bottle of mineral water, as commonly sold in Singapore and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: Bottled Water, NFS — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bottled water used as the primary nutrient reference. Mineral water is essentially pure water with trace dissolved minerals; macronutrient profile is identical to bottled water (zero energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate). Sodium value reflects typical mineral water mineral content per USDA data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Water — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water and mineral water contain no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore have no measurable glycemic index. By established nutritional convention, water is assigned GI = 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category = low. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that non-carbohydrate foods are not testable for GI.

hypertension.guidance: Mineral water contains approximately 2 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is nutritionally negligible and poses no concern for individuals managing hypertension; adequate daily water intake is beneficial for blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Mineral water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has no effect on blood glucose levels, making it the preferred hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mineral water contains no fat, cholesterol, or clinically significant sodium, and adequate hydration supports cardiovascular function; it is universally appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mineral water is naturally sourced or processed bottled water containing dissolved minerals such as calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, and trace elements. It is sold in sealed plastic or glass bottles, typically in 500 ml portions as commonly served in Singapore and food delivery contexts. Mineral water has no colour, no taste beyond a subtle mineral freshness, and no odour. It contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates, making it the most nutritionally neutral beverage available. The dissolved mineral content varies by brand and source but is generally low. Mineral water is universally suitable for all dietary patterns including vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, it is the preferred hydration beverage for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, as it exerts no glycemic, lipid, or sodium burden. It is known in Chinese as 矿泉水 (kuàng quán shuǐ), in Malay as air mineral, in Tamil as கனிம நீர், and in Hindi as मिनरल वाटर.
common_user_queries: Is mineral water good for diabetics?
Is mineral water the same as spring water?
Can I drink mineral water if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Mineral water is a well-characterised food item with a universally consistent nutrient profile (zero macronutrients) confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The nutrient values are not brand-specific and are stable across all mineral water products. GI convention for water is well-established in the literature.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as mineral water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. All vitamin fields are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (bottled water entry). iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mg, and all B-vitamins are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are naturally zero for water. Sodium 2 mg per 100 ml reflects typical mineral water mineral content per USDA data; actual sodium varies by brand. Potassium 1 mg and calcium 6 mg and magnesium 1 mg are representative typical values for mineral water per USDA SR Legacy. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. The 500 ml serving size is taken from the field survey listing context. Mineral water is listed under Chinese cuisine in the source data as it was served by a Chinese restaurant on field survey data; however, mineral water is a globally universal beverage with no specific regional origin.

improvement_actions_taken: Applied zero-macro justification rule: all zero core macros documented with 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' phrases in notes.
Applied zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule: glycemic_index set to 1, gi_category set to 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0.
Applied Singaporean cuisine multilingual naming rule: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names all populated.
Applied aliases consistency rule: verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Applied ontology match_type rule: used only 'exact' and 'closest_parent' values.
Sentinel values documentation: no -1 values used; all fields set to 0.0 or positive numeric values where data is available from USDA.
